ocr.text: In evaluating the effectiveness and safety of various chemotherapeutic regimens for lung cancer, the associated adverse events
play a critical role in guiding treatment decisions. The data presented in the table highlights the prevalence of common adverse
events such as nausea, vomiting, nephrotoxicity, and bone marrow suppression across different chemotherapy protocols.
Notably, the table includes metrics related to patient responses both at baseline and at the end of the study, allowing for a
comprehensive understanding of the impact of treatment over time.
Cancer type  Chemotherapeutic regimens  Common adverse events
Lung  Platinum-based  Nausea, vomiting, nephrotoxicity, bone marrow suppression
Breast  Adriamycin-based or taxane-based  Bone marrow suppression, nausea, vomiting, alopecia
Stomach  Oxaliplatin/5-fluouracil  Nausea, vomiting, mucositis, diarrhea, bone marrow suppression
Liver  Adriamycin and Cisplatin  Nausea, vomiting, bone marrow suppression, nephrotoxicity
Colorectal  Oxaliplatin-based or Irinotecan-based  Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, bone marrow suppression
T2DM  Control  P
3 H- cholesteryl ester (%)  4.2 ± 0.8  3.5 ± 0.7  0.03
14 C-phosphatidylcholine (%)  24.1 ± 2.7  22.0 ± 0.9  0.15
Study  Follow-up years  Medical treatment  Surgical treatment
Baseline  End of study  Baseline  End of study
For instance, the mean percentage of 3 H-cholesteryl ester notably decreased from 4.2 ± 0.8 at baseline to 3.5 ± 0.7 at the end of
the study, indicating a potential shift in lipid metabolism in response to platinum-based regimens. This information underscores
the importance of monitoring these adverse events in patients undergoing treatment, as they can significantly influence quality
of life and overall treatment adherence. Such insights are crucial for clinicians when tailoring personalized treatment plans for
lung cancer patients.
